figures.caption: Figure B.1: Final residue in a large domain for 1D Burgers. Upper: weak asymptotics; down: exact asymptotics; left: 𝜆 = 0.4; right: 𝜆 = 0.5.
Figure C.4: Spectral gap as a function of 𝛾

Figure C.2: Convergence time (of 𝑢2 mean) as a function of damping coefficient 𝛾. Left, Middle, Right: thresholds are 0.1, 0.5, 2.0, respectively. The takeaway from these plots is: large damping converges faster eventually (small threshold), while small damping converges faster initially (large threshold).
Figure 2.1: Comparison of the profile to the approximate steady state

Figure 3.13: Left: Notations of activations that flow through the network. Right: an activation function is parameterized as a B-spline, which allows switching between coarse-grained and fine-grained grids.
Figure 3.11: The Gaussian random field dataset. Test losses of MLP and KANs, with different scales and dimensions. Increasing the number of samples by 10x helps overfitting.
Figure 4.4: Left: the contour of log10 𝐴 for the high contrast example; right: the contour of 𝐴 for the rough media example.
Figure 4.5: Numerical results for the high contrast example. Left: 𝑒H versus 𝑚; right: 𝑒𝐿2 versus 𝑚.
Figure 3.1: Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) vs. Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs)
Figure 4.3: Numerical results for the periodic example. Left: 𝑒H versus 𝑚; right: 𝑒𝐿2 versus 𝑚.
Figure B.4: Final equation residues for 2D Boussinesq
Figure B.5: Trajectory of losses for 2D Boussinesq: 10000 Adam iterations followed by 40000 self-scaled Broyden iterations.
Figure 3.10: The Gaussian random field dataset. Training losses of MLP and KANs, with different scales and dimensions.
Figure 3.4: Knot dataset. Supervised mode (left): we rediscover DeepMind’s three important variables. Unsupervised mode (right): we discover three “new” relations without supervision.

Figure C.3: Darcy flow. Left: samples obtained from EKHMC (top) and EKS (bottom), compared with MCMC. Middle: Evolution of | |𝑢 | |𝐻−2 for EKHMC and EKS for different 𝐼 = 128, 512, 2048. Right: The same as the middle, but | |𝑢 | |𝐿2 instead of | |𝑢 | |𝐻−2 . EKHMC converges faster than EKS.

Figure 3.9: 1D wave dataset, where the target function has increasing amplitudes of different frequency modes. Under various hyperparameters, MLPs manifest severe spectral biases (top), while KANs do not (bottom). Note that the y axis (training steps) of MLP is 10 times that of KAN.

Figure 3.8: 1D wave dataset, where the target function has equal amplitudes of different frequency modes. Under various hyperparameters, MLPs manifest strong spectral biases (top), while KANs do not (bottom). Note that the y axis (training steps) of MLP is 10 times that of KAN.
Figure 2.4: Fitting the law of the normalization constants for 2D.
Figure B.3: Final profiles for 2D Boussinesq
Figure 3.6: Image fitting task (a PDE solution from PDEBench [342]). KAN outperforms baseline methods in terms of PSNR.
Figure 2.3: Fitting the law of the normalization constants. Left: (1/2 − 𝑐𝑙)𝜏 − 5/8 versus 𝜏; right: (𝑐𝑢 + 1)𝜏 − 1/4 versus 𝜏.
Figure 3.3: KANs are interpretable for simple symbolic tasks.
Figure 4.2: Two level mesh: a fraction

Figure 3.7: The PDE example. We plot L2 squared and H1 squared losses between the predicted solution and ground truth solution. First and second: training dynamics of losses. Third and fourth: scaling laws of losses against the number of parameters. KANs converge faster, achieve lower losses, and have steeper scaling laws than MLPs.
Figure 4.6: Numerical results for the mixed boundary and rough field example. Left: 𝑒H versus 𝑚; right: 𝑒𝐿2 versus 𝑚.
Figure B.2: Trajectory of losses for 1D Burgers. Upper: weak asymptotics; down: exact asymptotics; left: 𝜆 = 0.4; right: 𝜆 = 0.5.
Figure 3.12: Solving PDEs. 𝐿2 and 𝐻1 losses of MLP and KAN with different frequencies of the solution.
Figure 2.2: Plot of the residue multiplied by the rescaled time

Figure 3.2: Fitting the function 𝑓 (𝑥1, 𝑥2, 𝑥3, 𝑥4) = exp( 1 2 (sin(𝜋(𝑥2 1+𝑥 2 2))+sin(𝜋(𝑥2 3+ 𝑥2 4)))). (a) 3-Layer KAN admits smooth representations. (b) The 2-Layer KAN learns highly oscillatory representations. (c) The 3-layer KAN achieves lower losses and has a smaller train-test gap than the 2-layer KAN.
